content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

BAGATELOS & FADEM
THE INTERNATIONAL BUILDING
601 CALIFORNIA STREET
SUITE 1801
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108

BARRY FADEM
PETER A. BAGATELOS

TELEPHONE
(415) 982-7100
FAX
(415) 982-1085

January 7, 1991

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
Legal Division
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Use of Officeholder Funds for Governmental
Purpose

Dear Ms. Breeze:

This firm serves as legal counsel to Los Angeles City
Councilman Richard Alatorre. On behalf of Mr. Alatorre, I am
writing to request advice and seek confirmation of our
interpretation of Government Code Section 85802, subdivision (b).
A more detailed discussion follows.

As we have previously discussed by telephone, Councilman
Alatorre wishes to prepare a manuscript which generally analyzes
the history and meaning of the political ascendancy of Chicano
and Latino politicians in Los Angeles and California, and which
in particular examines the public service career of Mr. Alatorre
himself as a Chicano leader. Much of the material will be
autobiographical in nature, with a view toward providing
educational insight and a positive role model for Chicano and
Latino youth, and stressing the virtues of participation by
Hispanics in the political process.

To assist him in preparing this manuscript, Mr. Alatorre
wishes to employ the services of a writer/researcher who will
conduct interviews, compile a collection of personal photographs,
newspaper articles, and videotapes, and provide research and
secretarial services relevant to this work. Because Mr. Alatorre
will receive no compensation for the book, he would like to pay
for the services of the writer/researcher from two sources: (1)
the funds remaining in the "Citizens for Alatorre" account, a
pre-Proposition 73 candidate-controlled committee which retains
certain surplus funds now subject to the "personal use" law; and
(2) current "officeholder" funds. In addition, in the event that
these two sources of funds are insufficient to fully compensate
the writer/researcher, Mr. Alatorre may wish to provide
additional compensation from his personal funds and subsequently

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
January 7, 1991
Page -2-


reimburse himself with future "officeholder" funds, if any, in
the event that he is re-elected to his city council seat.

     In the opinion of this firm, the foregoing circumstances are
primarily governed by the provisions of Government Code Section
85802, subdivision (b), which provides that "campaign funds shall
not be used to pay for or reimburse the cost of professional
services unless the services are directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose." Although to our knowledge
the Commission has not yet defined the terms "political purpose"
and "governmental purpose" as used in this section, it is our
opinion that the expenditures described above would qualify as
either as "political" or "governmental" expenditures and would
therefore be permissible.

     The word "political" is defined in Funk & Wagner's Standard
Desk Dictionary in the following manner "1. Of or concerned with
government. 2. Of, relating to, or involved in politics. 3.
Characteristic of or similar to politics or politicians. 4.
Having an organized system of government." The term "politics" is
defined as follows "1. The science or art of government. 2. The
affairs or activities of those engaged in controlling or seeking
to control the government; also, the profession or area of activity of such persons. 3. The principles, aims, or policies of a government. 4. The acts or practices of those who seek any position of power or advantage. 5. Political opinions."

     The term "governmental" is defined in this dictionary by
reference to the term "government," which is defined as "1. The
authoritative administration of the affairs of the nation, state,
city, etc.; the jurisdiction exercised over the people; rule. 2.
The official governing body of a nation, community, etc. 3. The
system or established form by which a nation, etc., is controlled; democratic government. 4. Any governed territory, district, etc. 5. Control. (-governmental, ADJ.)"

     As the foregoing definitions disclose, the terms "political"
and "governmental" are substantially overlapping. However,
whether characterized as "political" or "governmental" in nature,
the work product of the relationship between Mr. Alatorre and the
writer/researcher he proposes to employ will certainly pertain to
the "art or science of government" and would therefore seem to
qualify under either definition if these terms are to given their
usual, commonly understood interpretation.

     We are aware that the Commission has previously applied the
definition of the term "political purpose" used in 2 Cal. Code of
Regs. Section 18215, and that if this definition of "political
purpose" were applied it would be necessary to determine whether
the expenditure was made in connection with a specific election.

Bianca Breeze, Esq.
January 7, 1991
Page -3-


However, in our view, it would be inappropriate to borrow this
definition and apply it to the interpretation of the "personal
use" law. The definition of "political purpose" used in the
existing regulation was designed for interpreting when a
particular payment constituted a reportable "contribution" or
"expenditure," and for determining whether an expenditure by
a candidate or officeholder was for personal use. As used in the
"personal use" law, we believe the word "political" must be given
its usual, commonly understood interpretation.

We also note that previous advice letters concerning the
"personal use" law appear to permit as "governmental"
expenditures payments for the business portion of a trip to
Eastern Europe by a county supervisor (FPPC File #I-90-556),
payments by a county sheriff for a trip to China (FPPC File #A-
90-474), and payment by a state senator for the hotel rooms,
meals, and transportation of panelists and invited guests
attending a conference on lobbying hosted by the senator (FPPC
File #A-90-596). If the foregoing expenditures are governmental
in nature, it would appear that a book designed to inform and
educate Hispanics about participation in the political process
would certainly qualify as "governmental" within the meaning of
this section.

I appreciate your attention to this matter and look forward
to receiving your advice regarding the application of this
decision under the foregoing circumstances. Please do not
hesitate to contact me if there is any additional information I
can provide.

Sincerely,

Wes Van Winkle
Of Counsel

WVW/mlq

On behalf of Mr. Alatorre, I am writing to request advice and seek confirmation of our interpretation of Government Code Section 85802, subdivision (b). A more detailed discussion follows. As we have previously discussed by telephone, Councilman Alatorre wishes to prepare a manuscript which generally analyzes the history and meaning of the political ascendancy of Chicano and Latino politicians in Los Angeles and California, and which in particular examines the public service career of Mr. Alatorre himself as a Chicano leader. Much of the material will be autobiographical in nature, with a view toward providing educational insight and a positive role model for Chicano and Latino youth, and stressing the virtues of participation by Hispanics in the political process. To assist him in preparing this manuscript, Mr. Alatorre wishes to employ the services of a writer/researcher who will conduct interviews, compile a collection of personal photographs, newspaper articles, and videotapes, and provide research and secretarial services relevant to this work. Because Mr. Alatorre will receive no compensation for the book, he would like to pay for the services of the writer/researcher from two sources: (1) the funds remaining in the "Citizens for Alatorre" account, a pre-Proposition 73 candidate-controlled committee which retains certain surplus funds now subject to the "personal use" law; and (2) current "officeholder" funds. In addition, in the event that these two sources of funds are insufficient to fully compensate the writer/researcher, Mr. Alatorre may wish to provide additional compensation from his personal funds and subsequently reimburse himself with future "officeholder" funds, if any, in the event that he is re-elected to his city council seat. In the opinion of this firm, the foregoing circumstances are primarily governed by the provisions of Government Code Section 85802, subdivision (b), which provides that "campaign funds shall not be used to pay for or reimburse the cost of professional services unless the services are directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose." Although to our knowledge the Commission has not yet defined the terms "political purpose" and "governmental purpose" as used in this section, it is our opinion that the expenditures described above would qualify either as "political" or "governmental" expenditures and would therefore be permissible. The word "political" is defined in Funk & Wagner's Standard Desk Dictionary in the following manner "1. Of or concerned with government. 2. Of, relating to, or involved in politics. 3. Characteristic of or similar to politics or politicians. 4. Having an organized system of government." The term "politics" is defined as follows "1. The science or art of government. 2. The affairs or activities of those engaged in controlling or seeking to control the government; also, the profession or area of activity of such persons.
